Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd (in liquidation)

Facts.Text: Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd was incorporated in Singapore on 28 June 2011.
Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd was at all material times part of the Inter-Pacific Group of companies.
Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd was 100% owned by Inter-Pacific Group Pte Ltd, a Singapore-incorporated company that was the ultimate holding company of the Inter-Pacific Group.
Zoe Cheung Lai Na was a director of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd from 28 June 2011.
Cheung Lai Ming, also known as Sara, became a director of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd on 15 February 2012.
Pek Chong Beng was appointed as a director of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd on 12 August 2019.
On 12 August 2019, both Dr Goh Jin Hian and Sara resigned from the board of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd.
Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd held a bunker supplier licence issued by the Maritime Port Authority of Singapore, which was required for supplying bunker fuel oil to customers.

Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd held a bunker craft operator licence issued by the Maritime Port Authority of Singapore, which was necessary for IPP to operate its own vessels for taking physical delivery of bunkers from suppliers and supplying to its own customers.

Around 13 June 2019, during enforcement checks conducted by the Maritime Port Authority of Singapore, tampering of the mass flow meter of a bunker tanker chartered by Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd was discovered.

On 27 June 2019, the Maritime Port Authority of Singapore placed Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd's bunker craft operator licence under temporary suspension following the discovery of mass flow meter tampering.

After the suspension of the bunker craft operator licence on 27 June 2019, Dr Goh Jin Hian took on a leading role in coordinating and directing IPP's response to the suspension, including writing to the Banks to inform them of the suspension and to provide them with updates on IPP's efforts at having the suspension lifted, as well as taking point in IPP's representations and negotiations with the MPA.

Following the suspension of the bunker craft operator licence, Dr Goh wrote to the Banks to inform them of the suspension and to provide them with updates on IPP's efforts at having the suspension lifted.

After the suspension of the bunker craft operator licence, Dr Goh took point in IPP's representations and negotiations with the MPA. This included, in particular, a show cause letter in which Dr Goh signed off as an 'Executive Director' of IPP.

Around 11 August 2019, Dr Goh met Zoe in Hong Kong. According to Dr Goh, he was informed at this meeting of Zoe's decision to place IPP into judicial management as IPP was unable to finance its payments to the Banks.
According to Dr Goh, he resigned from IPP on that same day.

On 22 August 2019, after meeting the Banks in Singapore, Dr Goh reported his findings by email to the Singapore Police Force. He claimed to have been told that IPP owed almost US$90m to SocGen and US$60m to Maybank in respect of financing that had been extended by the Banks for IPP's cargo trading operations.
In the period of around mid-June to July 2019, IPP drew down on the Facilities to the tune of some US$146,047,090.60 to finance cargo trading transactions.
In the period of around mid-June to July 2019, IPP drew down on the Facilities to the tune of some US$10,508,238.71 to finance bunkering transactions.
It was common ground between the parties that neither of these drawdowns (collectively, the June–July 2019 Drawdowns) were repaid to the Banks.
On or about 7 February 2018, an audit confirmation request relating to amounts of receivables due to IPP from Mercuria was signed by Dr Goh and sent to Mercuria.
On 17 July 2019, Dr Goh signed one of the confirmations of indebtedness to Maybank.
On 19 July 2019, Dr Goh signed one of the confirmations of indebtedness to Maybank.
Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd obtained a trade financing facility from Societe Generale, Singapore Branch, which could be used to finance both its bunker trading and cargo trading activities.
Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd obtained a trade financing facility from Malayan Banking Berhad, which could be used to finance its cargo trading activities.

On 13 November 2019, Dr Goh exchanged text messages with Wallace To asking for the management accounts for IPP and discussing gross profits/losses, net profit, operating costs, ship repairs and upkeep, and a possible acquisition by a Hong Kong listed company.
During mid-June to July 2019, IPP entered into a series of drawdowns under its trade financing facilities.

On 13 August 2019, Dr Goh emailed Zoe and others requesting that IPP and IPG pass board resolutions confirming his resignation and describing his previous role as a non-executive director appointed to represent his minority shareholder interests, and stating that it was important to indemnify him from management decisions and corporate actions of the companies.

On 13 August 2019, one day after his resignation, Dr Goh Jin Hian emailed Zoe and others requesting that Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd and Inter-Pacific Group Pte Ltd pass board resolutions confirming his resignation and describing his previous role as a non-executive director appointed to represent his minority shareholder interests, and stating that it was important to indemnify him from management decisions and corporate actions of the companies.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd's pre-dispute business operations, financing arrangements, and June–July 2019 drawdowns, the company incurred uncompensated liabilities to its trade financing banks arising from cargo and bunkering transactions.
Party_Details.Rule: A company that enters into trade financing arrangements with banks and draws down on those facilities for trading transactions is generally repay the financed amounts in accordance with the facility terms, and its net loss position depends on whether it receives corresponding assets or payments from counterparties and whether it has valid defences to repayment.
Party_Details.Application: Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd operated bunker trading and cargo trading businesses financed by facilities from Societe Generale, Singapore Branch and Malayan Banking Berhad. In mid-June to July 2019, the company drew down approximately US$146 million for cargo trading and about US$10.5 million for bunkering transactions. The drawdowns were not repaid before the company entered judicial management and later liquidation. After their appointment, the judicial managers identified large recorded receivables from cargo trading and contacted the named customers, who generally denied the underlying transactions. The purported carriers named on bills of lading associated with cargo trades involving Citus and Legend Six confirmed that those bills of lading were fraudulent documents. These circumstances Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd was left with substantial recorded liabilities to its financing banks from the June–July 2019 drawdowns without corresponding recoverable receivables from the recorded cargo trading counterparties.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court analysed the duties of directors under section 157(1) of the Companies Act 1967 and the common law, including the duty of skill, care and diligence and the duty to take into account the interests of creditors when a company is in financial difficulty. It considered the factual matrix of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd's bunker and cargo trading operations, its trade financing facilities with Societe Generale, Singapore Branch and Malayan Banking Berhad, and the June–July 2019 drawdowns. The judge examined whether the cargo trading transactions financed by the June–July 2019 cargo drawdowns were genuine or sham transactions. Relying on circumstantial evidence, including the absence of confirmations from Citus and Legend Six, the confirmation by purported carriers that bills of lading were fraudulent, and the denials by Mercuria, Minerva, Sinochem and Petco of most of the recorded cargo trades, the court concluded that the relevant cargo trading transactions were shams. The court then assessed the role and standard of care applicable to Dr Goh Jin Hian, determining that he functioned as an executive director of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd throughout the material period, notwithstanding his later attempt to characterise himself as a non-executive director. The judge found that board resolutions drafted after his resignation, which described him as a non-executive director with remote involvement, were created in the context of his email of 13 August 2019 seeking documents to indemnify him from management decisions, and treated these resolutions as self-serving and inconsistent with earlier records and his actual involvement. The court held that, as an executive director, Dr Goh was subject to at least the minimum objective standard of care requiring him to take reasonable steps to place himself in a position to guide and monitor the management of the company. The judge rejected arguments that his standard of care should be reduced on the basis that he was a non-executive director after July 2015, and also declined to increase the standard solely on the basis of his business experience and educational background beyond what would be expected of a reasonably diligent executive director in a similar company. On breach, the court examined whether Dr Goh was ignorant of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd's cargo trading business. It relied on his 22 August 2019 email to the Singapore Police Force, in which he described learning from the banks that the facilities were used for cargo trading and used language such as 'apparently' and asked the banks what the lines were given for, as indicating that he had not previously known of the cargo trading operations. The court also considered his statements in a 27 February 2020 interview with the judicial managers, where he repeatedly said that Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd was a bunker company, that there was no cargo trader in the Singapore office, and that he believed cargo trading was conducted at the level of a related Hong Kong entity. The judge rejected Dr Goh's explanations that the parties were using the term 'cargo' in different senses and that he had been referring only to a different mode of cargo trading, finding these explanations contrived and inconsistent with the context of the questions and his own words. The court further noted that when Dr Goh texted Chief Financial Officer Wallace To on 13 November 2019 asking for management accounts and discussing profitability in the context of a potential acquisition by a Hong Kong listed company, the discussion focused on bunker profits and chartering income, with no mention of cargo trading, which the court treated as consistent with his earlier lack of awareness of the cargo trading business. The judge concluded that Dr Goh had failed to take reasonable steps to inform himself of a major line of business of the company and to monitor its operations, and that this failure constituted a breach of his duty of care and diligence. The court then considered three specific 'red flags' that, in its view, should have prompted further inquiry by Dr Goh: the Mercuria audit confirmation request he signed in February 2018, the suspension of the bunker craft operator licence on 27 June 2019, and the Maybank confirmations of indebtedness he signed in July 2019. The judge analysed Dr Goh's responses to each of these events and found that he did not make adequate inquiries into Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd's financial position, the nature of the transactions being financed, or the extent of the company's exposure under the trade facilities. The court rejected Dr Goh's reliance on a 'trip-wire' theory that the banks' continued financing and repayment patterns obviated the need for him to review financial information or underlying transactions, holding that directors cannot delegate or outsource their core supervisory responsibilities to banks. The judge also rejected Dr Goh's reliance on the January 2019 management accounts as a sufficient basis for comfort, noting that the accounts did not reveal the true nature of the cargo trading receivables and that he did not probe further into the company's receivables position or the scale of its cargo trading activities. On the creditor duty, the court examined when Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd became balance sheet insolvent after discounting sham receivables and concluded that by June 2019 the company was in a financially parlous state such that the interests of creditors had to be taken into account. It held that Dr Goh, in allowing or not preventing the June–July 2019 drawdowns, did not adequately consider the interests of creditors, particularly given the company's deteriorating financial position and the absence of real assets backing the financed cargo trades. The judge then addressed whether Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd had suffered loss. For the bunkering drawdowns, the court accepted that the underlying bunkering transactions were genuine but considered whether the company nonetheless suffered loss because it had no realistic prospect of repaying the borrowings. For the cargo trading drawdowns, the court rejected arguments that the company had defences against the banks based on fraud or nullity exceptions to letters of credit or on alleged breaches of Quincecare duties by the banks. It held that the company remained liable to the banks for the financed amounts and that the absence of repayment did not mean that the company had not suffered loss, as a liability to a third party can constitute loss even before actual payment. The court also rejected the contention that no loss arose because the liquidators had not yet adjudicated the banks' proofs of debt, holding that the company's liability to the banks existed independently of the proof of debt process. The judge further rejected Dr Goh's 'round-tripping' theory that funds disbursed under the cargo trading drawdowns had been routed back to Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd, finding that the evidence did not support a conclusion that the company had been restored to a neutral position. On causation, the court considered whether, had Dr Goh taken reasonable steps in response to the red flags and to inform himself of the cargo trading business, the June–July 2019 drawdowns would have been avoided or reduced. It rejected Dr Goh's argument that any inquiries would have been thwarted by other individuals involved in the fraud, holding that a reasonably diligent director in his position could have taken steps such as insisting on direct access to financial records, engaging independent verification, or escalating concerns to other stakeholders, which would likely have prevented further drawdowns. The court also addressed Dr Goh's procedural objection that certain allegations, including his ignorance of the cargo trading business, were inadequately pleaded, and concluded that the pleadings and conduct of the trial gave him sufficient notice of the case he had to meet. Finally, the judge considered Dr Goh's application for relief under section 391 of the Companies Act, which allows the court to relieve a director from liability if he has acted honestly and reasonably and ought fairly to be excused. The court held that Dr Goh did not satisfy these criteria, given his failure over an extended period to acquaint himself with a major line of business, his inadequate response to clear warning signs, and his post-resignation attempts to characterise his role as non-executive and remote through board resolutions and correspondence. The court therefore declined to grant relief and ordered that Dr Goh pay compensation to Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd in the sum of US$146,047,099.60 in respect of the losses arising from his breaches of duty, together with orders on costs and disbursements.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Goh Jin Hian

Facts.Text: From 28 June 2011, Dr Goh Jin Hian was a director of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd.
On 12 August 2019, Dr Goh Jin Hian resigned as a director of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd.
Dr Goh Jin Hian was a director of Inter-Pacific Group Pte Ltd, the holding company of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd.
Dr Goh Jin Hian held a 15% shareholding in Inter-Pacific Group Pte Ltd, while Zoe held the remaining 85%.

Within the Inter-Pacific Group, Dr Goh Jin Hian was also a director of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Trading Pte Ltd, Pacific Energy 8 Pte Ltd, Pacific Energy 28 Pte Ltd, Pacific Energy 138 Pte Ltd, Pacific Energy 168 Pte Ltd, Pacific Energy 328 Pte Ltd, and Pacific Ship Management Pte Ltd.

On 20 July 2015, Dr Goh Jin Hian emailed Zoe and human resources manager Charlene stating his intention to change his role in Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd and to step down from executive responsibilities in view of his new role in New Silkroutes Group.

On 7 May 2015, Dr Goh Jin Hian wrote to the Maritime Port Authority of Singapore seeking approval to step down from his role in Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd while the company held bunker-related licences that had been granted on the condition of his shareholding and directorship.

On 9 June 2015, Dr Goh Jin Hian sent a further email to the Maritime Port Authority of Singapore regarding his proposed departure from Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd's board in light of licensing conditions.

From July 2015, the quantum of monthly payments from Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd to Dr Goh Jin Hian changed from S$30,000 to S$12,000 and the nature of the payments changed from salary to directors' fees, with Central Provident Fund contributions ceasing.
In 2017, an internal contact list of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd prepared by human resources manager Charlene described Dr Goh Jin Hian as an executive director of the company.
In 2018, an updated internal contact list of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd again described Dr Goh Jin Hian as an executive director, while reflecting changes in other staff positions.

After the Maritime Port Authority of Singapore suspended Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd's bunker craft operator licence on 27 June 2019, Dr Goh Jin Hian coordinated the company's response, including communications with the banks and the regulator and signing a show cause letter as executive director.

Around 11 August 2019, during a meeting in Hong Kong, Zoe informed Dr Goh Jin Hian that Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd would be placed into judicial management because it was unable to finance payments to its trade financing banks, and Dr Goh decided to resign from the company.

On 13 August 2019, Dr Goh Jin Hian emailed Zoe and others stating that he had resigned as director of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd and Inter-Pacific Group Pte Ltd on 12 August 2019 and requesting that board resolutions be prepared to confirm his resignation and to record that he had been a non-executive director appointed due to his minority shareholding, and that it was important to indemnify him from management decisions and corporate actions.

Following Dr Goh Jin Hian's 13 August 2019 email, undated board resolutions were prepared for Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd and Inter-Pacific Group Pte Ltd recording acceptance of his resignation and stating that he had been appointed as a non-executive director with remote involvement in day-to-day operations and appointed solely to represent his voting rights as a member.

On 22 August 2019, after meeting representatives of Societe Generale, Singapore Branch and Malayan Banking Berhad, Dr Goh Jin Hian emailed the Singapore Police Force stating that Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd owed almost US$90 million to Societe Generale and about US$69 million to Malayan Banking Berhad, that these amounts could not be explained by the bunkering business turnover, and that the banks had informed him that the facilities were used for cargo trading on a back-to-back basis with counterparties including Citus, Legend Six, Mercuria, and Sinochem.

In his 22 August 2019 email to the Singapore Police Force, Dr Goh Jin Hian wrote that he had asked the banks to tell him the nature of the bank lines and what they were given for, and reported that the banks dealt mainly with Wallace To and Cecilia for the transactions and that trading contracts were usually signed by Zoe and Stephen.

In the same 22 August 2019 email, Dr Goh Jin Hian informed the Singapore Police Force that he previously told them Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd was engaged in bunkering and that it now appeared that Zoe had been engaging in cargo trades, some of which might be fraudulent.

On 27 February 2020, during an interview with the judicial managers of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd, Dr Goh Jin Hian stated that he had assumed the company was in the bunker industry, that he signed bank documents believing they related to bunker or ex-wharf cargo from breaking bulk, and that he considered there to be no cargo trader in the Singapore office, with cargo trading activity associated with a related Hong Kong entity.

On 13 November 2019, in a text message exchange with Chief Financial Officer Wallace To, Dr Goh Jin Hian requested management accounts for Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd, discussed bunker gross profits and chartering income, asked about net profit and operating costs, and commented that if the company was not profitable it was unlikely to be acquired by a Hong Kong listed company.

On or about 7 February 2018, Dr Goh Jin Hian signed an audit confirmation request addressed to Mercuria Energy Trading Pte Ltd concerning receivables recorded as due from Mercuria to Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd.

On 17 July 2019, Dr Goh Jin Hian signed confirmations of indebtedness on behalf of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd in favour of Malayan Banking Berhad for amounts outstanding under the trade financing facility.

On 19 July 2019, Dr Goh Jin Hian signed further confirmations of indebtedness on behalf of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd in favour of Malayan Banking Berhad for amounts outstanding under the trade financing facility.

After the suspension of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd's bunker craft operator licence on 27 June 2019, Dr Goh Jin Hian wrote to the trade financing banks to inform them of the suspension and to provide updates on efforts to have the suspension lifted.

Following the suspension of the bunker craft operator licence, Dr Goh Jin Hian led Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd's representations and negotiations with the Maritime Port Authority of Singapore, including signing a show cause letter as executive director.
On 16 January 2019, Dr Goh Jin Hian was copied on an email from Chief Financial Officer Wallace To that enclosed Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd's management accounts for September 2018.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on Dr Goh Jin Hian’s pre-dispute conduct and roles in Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd and related entities, he failed to monitor the company’s operations and financing in relation to its bunker and cargo trading activities and the June–July 2019 drawdowns.
Party_Details.Rule: A director who participates in the management of a company is generally use reasonable diligence in overseeing the company’s affairs, including informing himself about the company’s lines of business, understanding significant financing arrangements, and responding appropriately to indications of financial or operational risk.
Party_Details.Application: From 2011 until his resignation on 12 August 2019, Dr Goh Jin Hian served as a director of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd and held directorships in its holding company and several related entities. He held a minority shareholding in the holding company and was involved in the group’s bunker-related operations, including interactions with the Maritime Port Authority of Singapore regarding licensing conditions. In mid-2015, he communicated an intention to step down from executive responsibilities and sought regulatory approval to leave his role, and his remuneration structure changed from salary to directors’ fees with reduced quantum and cessation of Central Provident Fund contributions. However, internal contact lists in 2017 and 2018 continued to describe him as an executive director, and he remained involved in key matters such as responding to the June 2019 suspension of the bunker craft operator licence, leading communications with the trade financing banks and the regulator, and signing a show cause letter as executive director. In February 2018, he signed an audit confirmation request to Mercuria Energy Trading Pte Ltd concerning receivables, and in July 2019 he signed confirmations of indebtedness to Malayan Banking Berhad for amounts outstanding under the trade financing facility. On 22 August 2019, after meeting the banks, he emailed the Singapore Police Force stating that he had just learned that Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd owed substantial sums to Societe Generale and Malayan Banking Berhad that could not be explained by the bunkering business turnover, and that the banks had informed him the facilities were used for cargo trading on a back-to-back basis with counterparties including Citus, Legend Six, Mercuria, and Sinochem. In a February 2020 interview with the judicial managers, he described his assumption that the company was in the bunker industry, indicated that he believed there was no cargo trader in the Singapore office, and associated cargo trading with a related Hong Kong entity. In November 2019, when requesting management accounts from the Chief Financial Officer, his discussion focused on bunker profits and chartering income in the context of a potential acquisition, without reference to cargo trading. These pre-dispute events Dr Goh remained involved in significant operational and financing matters, signed key financial documents, and communicated with regulators and banks, while his own statements later indicated that he had not been aware of the scale and nature of the cargo trading activities financed by the trade facilities.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court examined Dr Goh Jin Hian’s role and conduct as a director of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd to determine whether he met the standard of care and whether he complied with the duty to consider creditors’ interests when the company was in financial difficulty. It first addressed his contention that he had transitioned to a non-executive role from July 2015 due to his appointment as Group Chief Executive Officer of New Silkroutes Group and potential conflicts of interest. The judge considered evidence such as his July 2015 emails to Zoe and the human resources manager, changes in his remuneration and Central Provident Fund contributions, and his communications with the Maritime Port Authority of Singapore about stepping down. The court held that these materials showed an intention to change his role but did not establish that he actually ceased executive functions. The judge placed significant weight on internal contact lists from 2017 and 2018 prepared by the human resources manager, which continued to describe him as an executive director while reflecting other staff changes, and rejected his explanation that this was a mere template oversight. The court also scrutinised undated board resolutions prepared after his resignation that described him as a non-executive director with remote involvement and appointed solely to represent his voting rights. In light of his 13 August 2019 email requesting such resolutions and stating that it was important to indemnify him from management decisions and corporate actions, the judge inferred that the resolutions were crafted to support a narrative of limited involvement and to protect him from potential liability. The court noted inconsistencies between the resolutions and his own earlier account of his appointment being linked to licensing conditions, as well as the illogical reference to representing shareholder rights in Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd, which was wholly owned by Inter-Pacific Group Pte Ltd. The judge concluded that Dr Goh functioned as an executive director throughout the material period and was subject to the standard of a reasonably diligent executive director. On the question of knowledge and oversight, the court analysed Dr Goh’s 22 August 2019 email to the Singapore Police Force, in which he described learning from the banks that Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd owed almost US$90 million to Societe Generale and about US$69 million to Malayan Banking Berhad, that these amounts could not be explained by the bunkering business turnover, and that the banks had told him the facilities were used for cargo trading on a back-to-back basis with counterparties including Citus, Legend Six, Mercuria, and Sinochem. The judge interpreted his use of the term 'apparently' and his statement that he had asked the banks what the lines were given for as indicating that he had not previously known of the cargo trading operations. The court also noted that he had previously told the police that the company was engaged in bunkering and that he now reported that Zoe had been engaging in cargo trades, some of which might be fraudulent, which the judge treated as a compound discovery of both the existence of cargo trading and potential fraud. The court rejected Dr Goh’s explanation that he had only meant to convey discovery of fraudulent trades and not the existence of cargo trading itself. The judge further relied on the 27 February 2020 interview with the judicial managers, where Dr Goh repeatedly stated that Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd was a bunker company, that there was no cargo trader in the Singapore office, and that cargo trading was associated with a related Hong Kong entity. The court found that his statements that he assumed the company was in the bunker industry, that he signed bank documents believing they related to bunker or ex-wharf cargo from breaking bulk, and that any cargo trading would have been at the level of the Hong Kong entity, were inconsistent with his later claim that he had known of the cargo trading business. The judge rejected his argument that the parties were using the term 'cargo' in different senses and that he had been referring only to a different mode of cargo trading, finding that he had demonstrated an ability to distinguish between cargo and ex-wharf cargo and that his explanations were contrived. The court also considered the 13 November 2019 text exchange with Chief Financial Officer Wallace To, in which Dr Goh requested management accounts and discussed bunker profits, chartering income, net profit, and operating costs in the context of a potential acquisition by a Hong Kong listed company, with no mention of cargo trading. The judge treated this as further evidence that his focus and understanding of the business were centred on bunkering rather than cargo trading. On breach of the duty of care, the court held that Dr Goh failed to take reasonable steps to inform himself about a major line of business of the company and to monitor its operations, particularly given the scale of the cargo trading drawdowns and the exclusive use of the Malayan Banking Berhad facility for cargo trading. The judge found that his ignorance of the cargo trading business, as evidenced by his own communications and statements, was inconsistent with the minimum standard of care expected of a reasonably diligent executive director. The court then examined specific events that it characterised as red flags: the February 2018 Mercuria audit confirmation request he signed, the 27 June 2019 suspension of the bunker craft operator licence, and the July 2019 confirmations of indebtedness to Malayan Banking Berhad. It concluded that a reasonably diligent director in his position would have made further inquiries into the nature of the receivables, the company’s overall receivables position, the impact of the licence suspension on the company’s financial health, and the scale and purpose of the borrowings under the trade facilities. The judge rejected Dr Goh’s reliance on a 'trip-wire' theory that the banks’ continued financing and repayment patterns provided sufficient assurance of the company’s financial health and obviated the need for him to review financial information or underlying transactions. The court held that directors cannot rely on banks to perform their supervisory role and that Dr Goh’s failure to probe beyond the January 2019 management accounts and to investigate the large cargo trading drawdowns fell below the required standard. Regarding the creditor duty, the court determined that by June 2019 Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd was balance sheet insolvent once sham receivables were excluded, and that the company’s financial position continued to deteriorate with each additional drawdown. It held that Dr Goh, as a director, was required to take into account the interests of creditors when considering or allowing further borrowings. The judge rejected his argument that the banks were not creditors at the time of the drawdowns and that borrowing from them was in creditors’ interests because it increased the asset pool, finding that the company incurred substantial liabilities without corresponding real assets from the sham cargo trades. The court also rejected his contention that his liability should be limited to the specific drawdowns he was aware of through the Malayan Banking Berhad confirmations, holding that his broader failure to supervise and inquire contributed to the overall pattern of drawdowns. On loss and causation, the court rejected Dr Goh’s arguments that Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd suffered no loss because the bunkering transactions were genuine, because the company allegedly had defences against the banks based on fraud or nullity exceptions or Quincecare duties, because the liquidators had not yet adjudicated the banks’ proofs of debt, because the company had not yet repaid the banks, or because of alleged round-tripping of funds. The judge held that the company’s liabilities to the banks for the June–July 2019 cargo trading drawdowns constituted loss, that the company did not have viable defences that would eliminate those liabilities, and that the absence of repayment or proof adjudication did not negate the existence of loss. The court found that Dr Goh’s breaches of duty were a factual and legal cause of the company’s loss, concluding that had he taken reasonable steps in response to the red flags and to understand the cargo trading business, he could have prevented or reduced the June–July 2019 drawdowns. The judge rejected his argument that any inquiries would have been thwarted by others involved in the fraud, holding that a reasonably diligent director could have insisted on direct access to records, sought independent verification, or escalated concerns. Finally, the court considered Dr Goh’s application for relief under section 391 of the Companies Act, which allows the court to relieve a director from liability if he has acted honestly and reasonably and ought fairly to be excused. The judge concluded that Dr Goh did not meet these criteria, given his prolonged failure to acquaint himself with a major line of business, his inadequate response to clear warning signs, and his post-resignation efforts to characterise his role as non-executive and remote through board resolutions and correspondence. The court therefore declined to grant relief and held him liable to pay US$146,047,099.60 in compensation to Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd, together with orders on costs, including standard costs, an indemnity uplift, and disbursements.
